original_abstract: Bioactive peptides are integrated sequences within protein structures that remain latent until released through enzymatic hydrolysis, at which point they exert specific physiological functions. Recent research has increasingly focused on characterizing bioactive peptides derived from diverse plant, animal, and microbial sources. These molecules are typically produced via hydrolysis using plant- or microbe-derived enzymes, digestive proteases, or through fermentation with proteolytic starter cultures. The specific amino acid composition and sequence of these peptides dictate their functional versatility, which encompasses antioxidative, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, cholesterol-lowering, and antihypertensive properties, as well as roles in immune modulation and solute binding. Identification and isolation of these peptides from complex hydrolysates are generally achieved through membrane separation, various chromatographic techniques, and mass spectrometry. Ultimately, the successful integration of bioactive peptides into health-promoting or therapeutic applications hinges on verifying their biostability, bioavailability, and overall safety profiles.
humanized_abstract: Bioactive peptides are individual or sequences of amino acids which are embedded within the protein structure and remain undiscovered until they are release via enzymatic hydrolysis and are then responsible for a wide array of physiological activities. Bioactive peptides are gaining importance due to the increasing number of studies focusing on their release from plant, animal and microbial origin. They are produced using plant and microbial enzymes, digestive proteases or via fermentation using proteolytic starter culture. The various amino acid sequences and compositions of bioactive peptides are responsible for the variety of their applications in food industry such as antioxidative, antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, hypocholesterolemic and antihypertensive activities and immunomodulatory and solute binding properties. Bioactive peptides are extracted from hydrolysates using membrane separation techniques, chromatography and mass spectrometry and need to have stable, bioavailability and safe characteristics in order to be used as ingredients in functional food products and pharmaceuticals.
